title: Kidney Transplantation
authors: Jamil R. Azzi, Naoka Murakami, Anil K. Chandraker

items.title: Why is kidney transplantation the preferred treatment for ESKD?
items.content: Kidney transplantation is considered the optimal treatment for end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) due to its significant survival benefits and improved quality of life compared to chronic dialysis. The procedure has evolved with advancements in immunosuppressive therapies, leading to high 1-year survival rates.
keyPoints: Transplantation is the treatment of choice for ESKD.
>220,000 patients are living with a functioning kidney transplant in the U.S.
1-year survival rates: 98% for living-donor and 93% for deceased-donor allografts.
Survival benefit over dialysis becomes apparent within days to months post-transplant.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.title: What are the long-term survival rates and challenges of kidney transplantation?
items.content: While short-term outcomes for kidney transplants are favorable, long-term allograft survival has not improved significantly. The average graft survival is 19 years for living donors and 12 years for deceased donors. Chronic processes like interstitial fibrosis and vasculopathy contribute to eventual graft failure.
keyPoints: Long-term allograft survival: 19 years (living-donor), 12 years (deceased-donor).
Chronic issues leading to graft loss include interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, vasculopathy, and glomerulopathy.
Age-related mortality rates post-transplant are highest in the first year.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.title: Understanding Kidney Transplant Allocation: KDPI and New Policies
items.content: The allocation of kidney transplants in the U.S. has evolved to address donor shortages and improve equity. The introduction of the Kidney Donor Profile Index (KDPI) helps estimate graft failure risk, guiding organ allocation based on quality and recipient survival expectations. Additionally, new policies like distance-based allocation aim to reduce geographic disparities but have led to increased complexity in organ sharing.
keyPoints: KDPI score ranges from 0 to 100% to estimate graft failure risk.
KDPI <20% allocated to top 20% of recipients with highest expected survival.
KDPI >85% directed toward patients who benefit more from early transplantation despite lower quality.
Distance-based policy offers kidneys first to candidates within 250 nautical miles, aiming to reduce geographic disparities.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.title: Expanding the Donor Pool: Innovative Strategies and Their Impact
items.content: To address the critical shortage of kidney donors, several strategies have been implemented. These include using kidneys from donors after cardiac death (DCD), which now account for 30% of deceased donor transplants, and utilizing organs from HCV-positive donors due to advancements in antiviral therapies. The HOPE Act also facilitates organ donation from HIV-positive candidates.
keyPoints: DCD kidneys constitute 30% of total deceased kidney transplants.
HCV-positive donor transplants account for 9% of deceased kidney transplants since 2017.
HOPE Act authorizes organ donation from HIV-positive donors, with >100 transplants performed.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.title: Improving Access and Reducing Disparities in Kidney Transplantation
items.content: Efforts to improve access to kidney transplantation for minority populations include allowing B blood type candidates with low anti-A titer to receive A blood type organs. This policy aims to reduce wait times, particularly benefiting the African-American ESKD population. Additionally, xenotransplantation using genetically engineered pig kidneys is being explored as a potential solution to organ shortages.
keyPoints: B blood type candidates with low anti-A titer can receive A blood type organs to improve access and reduce disparities.
Three experimental kidney transplantations from genetically engineered pigs were performed in 2022.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.subtopic: RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])
items.title: Understanding the Categories and Use of DCD Kidneys in Transplantation
items.content: Kidneys from non-heart-beating donors, categorized as II to V, can be utilized for transplantation. However, categories III (awaiting cardiac arrest) and IV (cardiac arrest after brainstem death) are most commonly used. The survival rates of these kidneys have not been shown to be inferior compared to those from deceased-donor kidneys.
keyPoints: DCD kidney categories: II–V
Commonly used DCD categories for transplantation: III and IV
Survival comparison: Not inferior to deceased-donor kidneys
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])

items.subtopic: RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])
items.title: KDPI and DCD Kidneys: Balancing Risks and Benefits
items.content: Kidneys with a KDPI score >85% can be from DCD donors. Despite having poorer survival rates, these kidneys are allocated to patients who benefit more from early transplantation despite the risks associated with lower-quality organs.
keyPoints: KDPI score >85% indicates poorer kidney survival
DCD kidneys may have high KDPI scores
Allocation strategy: Benefits of early transplantation outweigh risks for certain patients
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.title: Importance of HLA Matching in Kidney Transplantation
items.content: HLA matching is crucial for selecting kidney donors to minimize the risk of organ rejection. The number of mismatches at A, B, and DR loci directly correlates with allograft survival; more mismatches increase rejection risk.
keyPoints: HLA genes are located on human chromosome 6 and are highly polymorphic.
The number of HLA antigen mismatches in A, B, and DR loci correlates with allograft survival.
Even HLA-identical renal allografts can be rejected due to non-HLA antigens.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.title: Role of Non-HLA Antigens in Kidney Transplant Rejection
items.content: Non-HLA antigens, including those at the LIMS-1 locus, can contribute to acute rejection risk. These antigens may cause immune responses that are refractory to conventional immunosuppressive therapy if prior sensitization has occurred.
keyPoints: Non-HLA minor antigens and ABH(O) antigens can elicit alloimmune responses.
Genetic polymorphisms in the LIMS-1 locus contribute to acute rejection risk.
Refractory immune responses may occur with prior exposure to non-HLA antigens.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.title: Evaluating Risks for Living Kidney Donors
items.content: Living kidney donors are evaluated to minimize harm, considering risks like hypertension and diabetes. The lifetime risk of ESKD is 0.3–0.4%, with a relative increase of 0.2–0.3% compared to healthy non-donors.
keyPoints: Donor evaluation excludes conditions that may cause morbidity or mortality post-donation.
Lifetime risk of ESKD in donors is 0.3–0.4%, increased by 0.2–0.3% relative to non-donors.
African-American donors have a higher risk of ESKD, and genetic screening for APOL1 alleles may be appropriate.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.title: Criteria for Deceased-Donor Kidney Use and Ischemic Time Considerations
items.content: Deceased donors must be free of malignant neoplastic disease, hepatitis, and HIV to prevent transmission to the recipient. However, HCV- and HIV-positive organs may sometimes be used under specific conditions. The risk of graft failure increases with elderly donors, those with acute kidney injury, or kidneys experiencing prolonged ischemia. In the U.S., deceased-donor kidneys can be maintained for up to 48 hours on cold pulsatile perfusion or ice before transplantation, though an ischemic time of less than 24 hours is preferred.
keyPoints: Deceased donors should be free from malignant neoplastic disease, hepatitis, and HIV.
HCV- and HIV-positive organs may sometimes be used under certain circumstances.
Increased risk of graft failure with elderly donors or those with acute kidney injury.
Kidneys can be maintained for up to 48 hours on cold pulsatile perfusion or ice.
<24 hour ischemic time is preferred.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.title: Importance of Crossmatching in Kidney Transplantation
items.content: Crossmatching is crucial to detect antibodies against donor antigens, which can cause allograft injury. Donor T lymphocytes are used for detecting circulating anti-class I (HLA-A and -B) antibodies. A positive cytotoxic crossmatch indicates preformed donor-specific anti-HLA class I antibodies, predicting hyperacute rejection, an absolute contraindication for transplantation.
keyPoints: Crossmatching detects antibodies against donor antigens to prevent allograft injury.
Donor T lymphocytes are used to detect circulating anti-class I (HLA-A and -B) antibodies.
Positive cytotoxic crossmatch indicates preformed donor-specific anti-HLA class I antibodies.
Hyperacute rejection is an absolute contraindication for kidney transplantation.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.title: Desensitization Strategies and Kidney Paired Donation Programs
items.content: Desensitization techniques, such as plasmapheresis and IVIG administration, reduce the risk of hyperacute rejection by lowering anti-donor antibody levels. Kidney paired donation programs facilitate compatible transplants for presensitized candidates by swapping living donor kidneys.
keyPoints: Desensitization reduces anti-donor antibodies using plasmapheresis and/or IVIG.
Reduces risk of hyperacute rejection post-transplantation.
Kidney paired donation programs swap living donor kidneys for compatible transplants.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.title: Mechanisms of T Cell-Mediated Kidney Transplant Rejection
items.content: T cell-mediated rejection occurs when recipient T lymphocytes react to donor HLA antigens within the transplanted kidney. CD4+ T cells respond to class II (HLA-DR) incompatibility, proliferating and releasing proinflammatory cytokines. CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocytes target class I (HLA-A, -B) antigens, leading to direct organ damage through cell lysis. Full activation of T cells requires both alloantigen recognition and costimulatory molecule engagement, activating calcineurin and transcription factors that upregulate IL-2 and interferon γ.
keyPoints: Recipient T lymphocytes respond to donor HLA antigens.
CD4+ T cells react to class II (HLA-DR) incompatibility.
CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocytes target class I (HLA-A, -B) antigens.
Full T-cell activation requires alloantigen recognition and costimulatory molecule engagement.
Activation of calcineurin leads to upregulation of IL-2 and interferon γ.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.title: Diagnosis and Pathophysiology of Antibody-Mediated Kidney Transplant Rejection
items.content: Antibody-mediated rejection is driven by circulating antibodies against donor antigens. Post-transplant, donor-derived antigens activate an alloimmune response in the recipient's lymph nodes. CD4+ follicular helper T cells (Tfh) promote B cell differentiation into plasma cells, which produce antibodies targeting HLA and non-HLA antigens. These antibodies can deposit in the kidney, causing injury through complement-dependent and independent mechanisms. C4d deposition in peritubular capillaries and glomerular basement membrane is a diagnostic marker of antibody-mediated rejection.
keyPoints: Antibody-mediated rejection involves circulating antibodies against donor antigens.
Donor-derived antigens activate an alloimmune response in lymph nodes.
CD4+ Tfh cells promote B cell differentiation into plasma cells.
Plasma cells produce antibodies targeting HLA and non-HLA antigens.
C4d deposition is a diagnostic marker of antibody-mediated rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.title: Why Induction Therapy Reduces Early Acute Rejection in Kidney Transplantation
items.content: Induction therapy is administered at the time of kidney transplantation to reduce the risk of early acute rejection and minimize or eliminate the use of steroids or calcineurin inhibitors, which have associated toxicities. This therapy involves using antibodies that can be either depleting or nondepleting.
keyPoints: Induction therapy is given at transplant time in most U.S. kidney recipients to reduce early acute rejection risk.
Therapy aims to minimize/eliminate steroids/calcineurin inhibitors and their toxicities.
Depleting agents like ATG cause lymphocyte depletion, with immune recovery taking months or years.
Monoclonal antibodies (e.g., Alemtuzumab) target specific lymphocyte subsets for standardized therapy.
Nondepleting agents target the 55-kDa alpha chain of IL-2 receptor on activated T cells to prevent early acute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.title: How Depleting and Nondepleting Agents Function in Induction Therapy
items.content: Induction therapy for kidney transplantation uses depleting agents like antithymocyte globulin (ATG) to cause lymphocyte depletion, impacting immune recovery time. Monoclonal antibodies such as Alemtuzumab target specific immune cells. Nondepleting agents focus on the IL-2 receptor's 55-kDa alpha chain on activated T cells to prevent early acute rejection without treating it.
keyPoints: Depleting agents like ATG cause lymphocyte depletion, impacting immune recovery time.
ATG is produced by injecting peripheral human lymphocytes into horses or rabbits.
Alemtuzumab targets CD52 on various immune cells for precise therapy.
Nondepleting agents target the 55-kDa alpha chain of IL-2 receptor on activated T cells.
These agents are used prophylactically to decrease early acute rejection rates with few side effects.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.title: Importance of TPMT Genotyping in Azathioprine Use
items.content: Azathioprine requires activation to thioguanine nucleotides and is metabolized by TPMT. Patients with two nonfunctional TPMT alleles face life-threatening myelosuppression, while those with one allele may experience significant side effects.
keyPoints: TPMT genotyping or phenotyping recommended before azathioprine treatment
Life-threatening myelosuppression in patients with two nonfunctional TPMT alleles
Significant side effects possible with one nonfunctional TPMT allele
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.title: Tacrolimus vs. Cyclosporine: Side Effects and Monitoring
items.content: Both tacrolimus and cyclosporine are CNIs with nephrotoxicity as a major concern. Tacrolimus, unlike cyclosporine, does not cause hirsutism or gingival hyperplasia but is associated with hair loss and more frequent posttransplant diabetes mellitus.
keyPoints: Tacrolimus has similar immunosuppressive action to cyclosporine
Nephrotoxicity is a major concern for both CNIs
Tacrolimus can cause hair loss, not hirsutism or gingival hyperplasia
Posttransplant diabetes mellitus more common with tacrolimus
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.title: Belatacept as an Alternative to CNIs in Kidney Transplants
items.content: Belatacept, a co-stimulatory blocking antibody, is used as an alternative to CNIs. It has shown improved patient and graft survival at 7 years compared to cyclosporine, despite higher short-term acute rejection rates.
keyPoints: Belatacept binds CD80/CD86 on antigen-presenting cells
FDA approved for kidney transplant recipients
Monthly intravenous infusion required
Improved patient and graft survival at 7 years in BENEFIT trial
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.title: Timing of Hemodialysis Pre-Transplantation to Prevent Cardiac Arrhythmias
items.content: Hemodialysis should be performed within 48 hours prior to kidney transplantation surgery to control serum potassium levels, preventing cardiac arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Perform hemodialysis within 48 hours before surgery.
Control serum potassium to prevent cardiac arrhythmias.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.title: Indications for Allograft Biopsy in Post-Transplant Monitoring
items.content: Allograft biopsy is crucial when there's slow recovery or oliguria post-transplant, as it helps distinguish between acute tubular injury and rejection.
keyPoints: Biopsy indicated with slow recovery or oliguria.
Distinguishes acute tubular injury from rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.title: Approach to Diagnosing and Treating Acute T Cell-Mediated Rejection
items.content: Acute T cell-mediated rejection is diagnosed via biopsy showing immune cell infiltration, treated initially with high-dose steroids; non-response suggests need for antibody therapy.
keyPoints: Diagnosis by Banff classification of immune cell infiltration.
Initial treatment: IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg daily for 3 days.
Non-response indicates need for antibody therapy, e.g., ATG.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.title: Cardiovascular Events as Leading Cause of Death in Kidney Transplant Recipients
items.content: Cardiovascular events are the predominant cause of mortality among kidney transplant recipients, accounting for 29% of deaths. This highlights the importance of cardiovascular risk management and monitoring in this population.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular events account for 29% of deaths post-kidney transplantation.
Emphasizes need for vigilant cardiovascular risk assessment and management.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Management Strategies for CMV Infection in Kidney Transplant Recipients
items.content: CMV infection is a significant post-transplant complication, with seronegative recipients of seropositive donors at highest risk. Diagnosis involves plasma viral load and IgM antibody rise. Valganciclovir is effective for both prophylaxis and treatment.
keyPoints: Seronegative recipients of seropositive donors are at highest CMV infection risk.
Diagnosis via plasma viral load and rise in IgM antibodies to CMV.
Valganciclovir used effectively for prophylaxis and treatment.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Chronic Allograft Dysfunction: Causes and Management
items.content: Chronic renal transplant dysfunction is multifactorial, often resulting from chronic active antibody-mediated rejection, recurrent glomerular disease, hypertension, CNI nephrotoxicity, or secondary focal glomerulosclerosis. Chronic vascular changes such as intimal proliferation and medial hypertrophy are common. Management includes controlling systemic and intrarenal hypertension with calcium channel blockers to slow progression.
keyPoints: Chronic renal transplant dysfunction is caused by factors like chronic active antibody-mediated rejection, recurrent glomerular disease, hypertension, CNI nephrotoxicity, or secondary focal sclerosis.
Chronic vascular changes include intimal proliferation and medial hypertrophy.
Calcium channel blockers can beneficially influence the progression of chronic allograft dysfunction.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Malignancy Risk in Immunosuppressed Transplant Patients
items.content: Patients on immunosuppressive therapy have a significantly increased risk of malignancies, with an incidence rate 5–6%, or ~100 times greater than the general population. Skin cancers are most common, necessitating surveillance and UV protection. Posttransplant lymphoproliferative disease is associated with Epstein-Barr virus and has poor prognosis. Malignancy risks correlate with immunosuppressive load and time since transplantation.
keyPoints: Incidence of tumors in immunosuppressed patients is 5–6%, ~100 times greater than the general population.
Most common malignancies are skin cancers; surveillance and UV protection are crucial.
Posttransplant lymphoproliferative disease often linked to Epstein-Barr virus with poor prognosis.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Cardiovascular Complications in Transplant Recipients
items.content: Transplant recipients, especially diabetics, face higher mortality from myocardial infarction and stroke. Contributing factors include glucocorticoids, sirolimus, and hypertension. Coronary artery and peripheral vascular diseases are prevalent. Over 30% of kidney transplant recipient deaths are due to cardiovascular disease. Management focuses on strict control of blood pressure, sugar, and lipid levels.
keyPoints: High incidence of death from myocardial infarction and stroke in transplant patients, especially diabetics.
>30% of kidney transplant recipient mortality is attributable to cardiovascular disease.
Management includes strict control of blood pressure (120–130/70–80 mmHg), sugar, and lipid levels.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which immunosuppressive therapies were introduced in the 1960s that enabled kidney transplantation across nonidentical individuals?
items.explanation: The introduction of azathioprine and prednisone in the 1960s established kidney transplantation across nonidentical individuals.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.question: What is the approximate 1-year survival rate for deceased-donor allografts in the United States?
items.explanation: The 1-year survival rates for deceased-donor allografts are approximately 93%.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.question: What is the average allograft survival time for living-donor kidney transplants?
items.explanation: The average allograft survival time for living-donor grafts is approximately 19 years.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.question: What was the primary purpose of implementing the new kidney allocation system in the United States in 2014?
options: To increase the number of living-donor transplants.
To offer transplantation opportunities to highly sensitized patients and prioritize high-quality organs for those expected to survive longest.
To reduce the overall cost of kidney transplantation procedures.
To eliminate geographic disparities in access to kidney transplantation.
items.explanation: The new allocation system aimed to offer opportunities to highly sensitized patients and prioritize high-quality organs for those expected to survive longest.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.question: What is the Kidney Donor Profile Index (KDPI) used for?
options: To estimate the potential risk of graft failure after kidney transplant.
To determine the eligibility criteria for living donors.
To calculate the cost-effectiveness of a kidney transplant.
To assess the financial burden on patients post-transplant.
items.explanation: The KDPI is used to estimate the potential risk of graft failure after kidney transplant based on donor factors.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.question: Which policy was introduced in 2021 to address geographic disparities in kidney transplantation?
options: A policy allowing kidneys from donors with hepatitis C virus (HCV) to be transplanted.
A distance-based kidney allocation policy offering organs first to candidates within 250 nautical miles of the donor hospital.
The HOPE Act, authorizing organ donation from HIV-positive candidates.
A policy prioritizing kidneys with a KDPI <20% for all recipients.
items.explanation: In 2021, a distance-based kidney allocation policy was introduced to address geographic disparities by offering organs first to nearby transplant hospital candidates.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.question: What percentage of total deceased kidney transplants in recent years have been from donors after cardiac death (DCD)?
items.explanation: Kidneys from donors after cardiac death now make up 30% of total deceased kidney transplants.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.question: Which categories of non-heart-beating donors are kidneys commonly used from for transplantation?
items.explanation: Kidneys are commonly used from categories III (Awaiting cardiac arrest) and IV (Cardiac arrest after brainstem death).

items.question: What is an absolute contraindication to kidney transplantation?
options: Chronic illness with predicted survival >2 years
items.explanation: Active substance abuse is an absolute contraindication to kidney transplantation.

items.question: What is the minimum cancer-free wait time recommended before kidney transplantation for patients with a history of malignancy?
items.explanation: A cancer-free wait time of 2–5 years is recommended depending on the type and stage of malignancy.

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.question: Which technique is currently almost universally used for HLA typing in kidney transplantation?
options: Serologic techniques using recipient sera and leukocytes with known serotypes.
Molecular typing of HLA by genomic sequencing.
Flow cytometry analysis of HLA expression.
items.explanation: Currently, molecular typing of HLA by genomic sequencing is almost universally used.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.question: Which antigens are not expressed on lymphocytes but can elicit an alloimmune response?
items.explanation: The ABH(O) and endothelial antigens are not expressed on lymphocytes but can elicit an alloimmune response.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.question: What is the correlation between HLA antigen mismatches and allograft survival?
options: More mismatches correlate with longer allograft survival.
The number of mismatches correlates with a higher risk of allograft rejection.
Mismatches have no impact on allograft survival.
items.explanation: The more HLA antigen mismatches, the higher is the risk of allograft rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.question: What can contribute to the risk of acute rejection besides HLA antigens?
options: Non-HLA antigen polymorphisms in the LIMS-1 locus.
items.explanation: Non-HLA antigen polymorphisms in the LIMS-1 locus can contribute to the risk of acute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.question: Which of the following is considered an absolute immunologic contraindication for kidney transplantation?
options: Presence of preformed donor-specific anti-HLA class I antibodies detected by a positive cytotoxic crossmatch.
Use of kidneys from donors with acute kidney injury.
Presence of non-HLA antigens restricted to endothelium and monocytes.
items.explanation: A positive cytotoxic crossmatch indicating preformed donor-specific anti-HLA class I antibodies is an absolute contraindication for kidney transplantation due to the risk of hyperacute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.question: What is a potential cause of allograft injury after kidney transplantation?
options: Presence of antibodies against donor antigens.
Use of kidneys from elderly donors.
Absence of HLA class II expression on T lymphocytes.
items.explanation: The presence of antibodies against donor antigens, either HLA or non-HLA, can cause allograft injury after transplantation.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.question: Which method is NOT part of clinical care for maintaining deceased-donor kidneys before transplantation?
items.explanation: Normothermic perfusion of donated organs has been studied but is not part of clinical care as of yet.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.question: What is the preferred ischemic time for deceased-donor kidneys to allow adequate preparation before transplantation?
items.explanation: An ischemic time of less than 24 hours is preferred, allowing adequate time for typing, cross-matching, transportation, and selection issues.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.question: Which type of T cells primarily respond to class II (HLA-DR) antigens during kidney transplant rejection?
items.explanation: CD4+ lymphocytes respond to class II (HLA-DR) incompatibility by proliferating and releasing proinflammatory cytokines.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.question: What is a diagnostic criterion for antibody-mediated rejection in kidney transplant recipients?
options: C4d deposition in peritubular capillaries and glomerular basement membrane
IL-2 signaling through the target of rapamycin (TOR)
items.explanation: C4d deposition in peritubular capillaries and glomerular basement membrane is a diagnostic criterion for antibody-mediated rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.question: Which signaling pathway is involved in the activation of T cells during kidney transplant rejection?
options: Calcineurin kinase activity leading to transcription factor activation
CD80 and CD86 engagement on plasma cells
items.explanation: Signaling through both T-cell receptor binding and costimulatory molecules induces calcineurin kinase activity, leading to transcription factor activation.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.question: Which of the following agents is a lymphocyte-depleting agent used in induction therapy for kidney transplant recipients?
items.explanation: Antithymocyte globulin (ATG) is a lymphocyte-depleting agent.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.question: Which monoclonal antibody targets CD52 in induction therapy for kidney transplant recipients?
items.explanation: Alemtuzumab is directed against CD52.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.question: Which of the following agents targets the IL-2 receptor alpha chain in induction therapy?
items.explanation: The IL-2 receptor alpha chain antibody targets the 55-kDa alpha chain of the IL-2 receptor.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.question: Which immunosuppressive agent is a co-stimulatory blocking antibody used as an alternative to long-term toxic calcineurin inhibitor therapy?
items.explanation: Belatacept is a co-stimulatory blocking antibody used as an alternative to long-term toxic calcineurin inhibitor therapy.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.question: Which of the following agents inhibits transcription of IL-2 and other proinflammatory cytokines by acting on the calcineurin pathway?
items.explanation: Cyclosporine acts on the calcineurin pathway to inhibit transcription of IL-2 and other proinflammatory cytokines.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.question: Which immunosuppressive agent is associated with a higher incidence of posttransplant diabetes mellitus?
items.explanation: Posttransplant diabetes mellitus more commonly occurs with tacrolimus.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended practice before starting treatment with azathioprine?
items.explanation: The FDA recommends TPMT genotyping or phenotyping before starting treatment with azathioprine.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.question: Where is the kidney allograft usually placed during transplantation surgery?
options: In the recipient's iliac fossa using a retroperitoneal approach.
In the recipient's abdominal cavity without specific placement.
Attached to the recipient's native kidneys.
Directly connected to the recipient's heart.
items.explanation: The kidney allograft is usually placed in the recipient's iliac fossa using a retroperitoneal approach.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.question: What is commonly used to diagnose acute T cell-mediated rejection?
options: Allograft biopsy according to the Banff classification.
Noninvasive biomarkers such as circulating donor-derived cell-free DNA.
items.explanation: Acute T cell-mediated rejection is diagnosed by allograft biopsy and classified using the Banff criteria.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.question: What treatment is indicated for antibody-mediated rejection?
options: High-dose steroids such as IV methylprednisolone.
Plasmapheresis, IVIG, anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (rituximab), and bortezomib.
Immediate nephrectomy of the native kidney.
items.explanation: Antibody-mediated rejection is treated with plasmapheresis, IVIG, anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (rituximab), and bortezomib.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosing acute rejection in kidney transplant recipients?
options: Clinical evidence such as fever and swelling.
Doppler ultrasonography to assess blood flow.
Noninvasive biomarkers like urine chemokine markers.
items.explanation: The gold standard for diagnosing acute rejection is an allograft biopsy.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.question: Which of the following is considered a critical opportunistic infection in kidney transplant recipients?
items.explanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii is a rare but critical opportunistic infection in kidney transplant recipients.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the treatment of choice for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia?
items.explanation: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is the treatment of choice for Pneumocystis jirovecii.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which virus reactivation in kidney transplant recipients can lead to progressive fibrosis and loss of the graft if untreated?
items.explanation: Reactivation of BK virus, if left untreated, can lead to progressive fibrosis and loss of the graft.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which condition is NOT listed as a cause of chronic renal transplant dysfunction?
items.explanation: Diabetic nephropathy is not listed as a cause of chronic renal transplant dysfunction in the passage.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.question: What type of biopsy can distinguish subacute cellular rejection from recurrent disease or secondary focal sclerosis?
items.explanation: The passage states that kidney allograft biopsy can distinguish these conditions.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.question: What is the most common malignancy in patients on immunosuppressive therapy?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that skin and lip cancers are the most common malignancies in these patients.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.question: What is a risk associated with cancer immunotherapy in transplant recipients?
items.explanation: The passage states that cancer immunotherapy is associated with a high risk of allograft rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Acute rejection is now a common cause of kidney transplant loss.
items.explanation: The loss of kidney transplant due to acute rejection is now a rare event.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.statement: Transplantation results in an improved life expectancy compared to patients who remain on dialysis.
items.explanation: Overall, transplantation results in an improved life expectancy with a higher quality of life compared to patients who remain on dialysis.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.statement: The HOPE Act has facilitated over 100 organ donations from HIV-positive candidates.
items.explanation: The HOPE Act authorized organ donation from HIV-positive candidates, resulting in more than 100 transplants.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.statement: At the 5- and 10-year follow-up, allograft survival rates are higher for deceased-donor kidneys compared to living-donor kidneys.
items.explanation: There is a steeper decline in survival of those with deceased-donor kidneys at 5- and 10-year follow-up.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.subtopic: RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])
items.statement: Kidneys from non-heart-beating donors have been shown to have inferior survival compared to deceased-donor kidneys.
items.explanation: The survival of kidneys from non-heart-beating donors has not been shown to be inferior to that of deceased-donor kidneys.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])

items.subtopic: RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])
items.statement: A high Kidney Donor Profile Index (KDPI) score (>85%) is associated with poorer kidney survival.
items.explanation: High KDPI kidneys have been shown to have a poorer survival.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECIPIENT EVALUATION | TABLE 325-1 Definition of a Non-Heart-Beating Donor (Donation After Cardiac Death $ ^{a} $ [DCD])

items.subtopic: TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION
items.statement: HLA antigens are not polymorphic and do not serve as immunologic targets in organ rejection.
items.explanation: HLA is highly polymorphic and can be an immunologic target of organ rejection when mismatched.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.statement: Living kidney donors have a higher risk of ESKD compared to the general population.
items.explanation: The risk of ESKD after kidney donation is not greater than that of the general population.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.statement: A positive cytotoxic crossmatch with donor T lymphocytes indicates the presence of preformed anti-HLA class II antibodies.
items.explanation: A positive cytotoxic crossmatch indicates preformed donor-specific anti-HLA class I antibodies, not class II.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.statement: Desensitization prior to transplantation can reduce the risk of hyperacute rejection.
items.explanation: Desensitization using plasmapheresis and/or IVIG reduces anti-donor antibodies, lowering the risk of hyperacute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.statement: T cell-mediated rejection in kidney transplants involves direct contact and lysis of donor target cells by CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocytes.
items.explanation: CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocytes cause organ damage through direct contact and lysis of donor target cells.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.statement: Immunosuppressive drugs used in kidney transplant recipients are specific to immune responses against the transplanted organ only.
items.explanation: Immunosuppressive therapies suppress all immune responses nonspecifically, including those to exogenous pathogens and even malignant tumors.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOLOGY OF REJECTION

items.statement: Depleting agents in induction therapy include antithymocyte globulin (ATG).
items.explanation: Antithymocyte globulin (ATG) is a lymphocyte-depleting agent used in induction therapy.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.statement: Nondepleting agents are primarily used for the treatment of acute rejection post-transplant.
items.explanation: Nondepleting agents target IL-2 receptor alpha chain and are used as prophylaxis, not treatment, for acute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.statement: Mycophenolate mofetil and mycophenolate sodium are metabolized to mycophenolic acid, which has a similar mode of action as azathioprine.
items.explanation: Both agents are metabolized to mycophenolic acid, which inhibits T-cell proliferation like azathioprine.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.statement: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus both produce hirsutism as a side effect.
items.explanation: Tacrolimus does not produce hirsutism, unlike cyclosporine.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.statement: Thrombosis of the renal vein in kidney transplant recipients is common and usually irreversible.
items.explanation: Renal vein thrombosis occurs rarely and may be reversible if intervention is prompt.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.statement: Acute tubular injury in the allograft can lead to postoperative diuresis.
items.explanation: Acute tubular injury due to ischemia accounts for postoperative diuresis.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.statement: CMV infection is associated with increased morbidity and mortality in kidney transplant recipients.
items.explanation: CMV infection is a serious complication after kidney transplantation associated with increased morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Acyclovir is the treatment of choice for Pneumocystis jirovecii.
items.explanation: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is the treatment of choice for Pneumocystis jirovecii, not Acyclovir.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MANAGEMENT OF CHRONIC COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: The incidence of tumors in patients on immunosuppressive therapy is approximately the same as that in the general population.
items.explanation: The passage states that the incidence is 5–6%, or ~100 times greater than that in the general population.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Hypercalcemia after transplantation may indicate failure of hyperplastic parathyroid glands to regress.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that hypercalcemia can indicate this condition.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.assertion: The success rate at the 1-year mark for deceased-donor allografts improved after the introduction of calcineurin inhibitors.
items.reason: Calcineurin inhibitors were introduced during the 1970s and 1980s, which led to marked improvements in the 1-year survival rates for these transplants.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · Kidney Transplantation

items.assertion: The KDPI score helps allocate kidneys based on expected posttransplant survival.
items.reason: Lower KDPI values are associated with higher expected posttransplant survival.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.assertion: The introduction of direct-acting antiviral therapies for HCV has reduced the number of kidney transplants.
items.reason: Transplantation from HCV-positive donors to both HCV-positive and -negative recipients has increased since 2017.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; transplantation numbers have not decreased. The reason is true, as the practice has indeed become more common.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · RECENT ACTIVITY AND RESULTS

items.assertion: Selective renal arteriography is essential in living donor evaluation.
items.reason: It reveals any anatomic anomaly and assesses size imbalance and laterality of donor kidneys.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why selective renal arteriography is essential.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.assertion: Financial incentives for kidney donors are encouraged to increase living donations.
items.reason: Removing financial disincentives reduces barriers toward living donation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because financial and nonfinancial conflicts of interest between kidney donors and recipients are strictly prohibited, although removing financial disincentives is accepted.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · TISSUE TYPING AND CLINICAL IMMUNOGENETICS | LIVING-DONOR EVALUATION

items.assertion: Eplet mismatches in class II HLA DQ loci are important risk factors for acute rejection.
items.reason: Eplets are short sequences of polymorphic amino acids on the surface of HLA antigens recognized by antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and eplet mismatches being recognized by antibodies explain their role as risk factors for acute rejection.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.assertion: T cells used in crossmatching are related to the risk of T cell-mediated rejection.
items.reason: T cells express class I but not class II HLA and are used as a surrogate target for detecting anti-class I antibodies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; using T cells in crossmatching is unrelated to the risk of T cell-mediated rejection, though the reason is true.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · DECEASED-DONOR EVALUATION | PRESENSITIZATION

items.assertion: Alemtuzumab is a monoclonal antibody used in induction therapy.
items.reason: Alemtuzumab targets CD52, which is widely expressed on immune cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Alemtuzumab is used.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE TREATMENT | INDUCTION THERAPY

items.assertion: Belatacept is given monthly as an intravenous infusion for kidney transplant recipients.
items.reason: It binds to costimulatory ligands on antigen-presenting cells, leading to T-cell anergy and apoptosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why belatacept is administered this way.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.assertion: The use of sirolimus or everolimus during the perisurgical period is preferred due to their minimal impact on wound healing.
items.reason: mTOR inhibitors are associated with poor wound healing, making them less preferable during the perisurgical period.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because mTOR inhibitors like sirolimus and everolimus are not preferred due to poor wound healing.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · MAINTENANCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.assertion: The recipient's serum creatinine should start to fall as the allograft starts to function.
items.reason: Recovery of kidney function typically occurs within 2 weeks, although it can take up to 6 weeks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why serum creatinine falls as allograft function improves.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION
items.assertion: A rise in serum creatinine is always indicative of rejection.
items.reason: Rejection may present only with a rise in serum creatinine, with or without reduction in urine volume.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because other causes such as acute tubular injury can also cause a rise in serum creatinine.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CLINICAL COURSE AND MANAGEMENT OF THE RECIPIENT | MANAGEMENT OF REJECTION

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: Strict control of blood pressure and blood sugar is essential in kidney transplant recipients.
items.reason: This population has a high prevalence of coronary artery and peripheral vascular diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why strict control is essential.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: Most transplant patients have robust production of erythropoietin posttransplant.
items.reason: Anemia in the posttransplant period is often due to bone marrow-suppressant immunosuppressive medications.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Kidney Transplantation · CHRONIC LESIONS OF THE TRANSPLANTED KIDNEY | MALIGNANCY | OTHER COMPLICATIONS
